event_blocks.summary: An upbeat electronic music track begins as the video transitions to a man standing behind a table laden with computer hardware boxes against a vibrant blue background. He introduces the 'Skydive channel' and their goal to build the 'best $2,000 gaming PC in 2023,' correcting an initial mention of $3,000 after an off-screen voice interjects. The components include a Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, an AMD Ryzen 7 CPU, an ASUS ROG Strix GeForce RTX 4090, a Corsair H100i RGB Platinum liquid cooler, a Seasonic PRIME 850W power supply, and a white PC case. The man explains that this build offers 'the best of the best' without excessive spending, with the CPU and GPU capable of crushing 4K gaming with ray tracing and performing excellently for content creation and streaming. Brief gameplay footage of a racing game and a first-person shooter is shown, followed by a fully assembled white PC with RGB lighting. The man then details the build process, starting with installing components into the white PC case and showing Windows and driver installation. He emphasizes that links to all parts are in the description and humorously states that only a banana, scissors, and a USB drive are needed for assembly. He unboxes the AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D CPU, calling it the 'best gaming CPU' for 2023, noting that 8 cores are sufficient for high-end gaming. He explains the need for an aftermarket cooler, showcasing the Enermax Aquafusion ADX liquid cooler, chosen for its white aesthetic. The Gigabyte AORUS B650 Elite AX Ice motherboard is presented, highlighting its Wi-Fi capability, and then installed into the PC case. The CPU is meticulously installed into the motherboard socket, followed by the Enermax AIO cooler. White G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM, specifically a 32GB kit at 6000 MHz with CL30 latency, is installed, chosen for its white color and good value. The man then discusses the T-Force Cardea A440 Pro 2TB M.2 SSD, a PCIe Gen 4.0 drive, as a high-quality, premium option that avoids overspending. The SSD is installed into the motherboard, and the man continues discussing read and write speeds while holding a small component in a clear plastic bag.

Racing Game Footage.description: In-game footage of a white race car on a rainy track, with rain effects and a heads-up display showing lap time and position.

Seasonic PRIME 850W Power Supply Box.description: Black box with 'CENTURY G5 850W' and '80 PLUS GOLD' certification visible.

AMD Ryzen 7 CPU Box.description: Small black and orange box with 'AMD RYZEN 7' branding, containing the '7800X3D' CPU which the man identifies and prepares for installation, noting it doesn't come with a stock cooler.

Man.description: A young man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, standing behind a table with computer components, speaking directly to the camera with a clear, professional, and knowledgeable voice, explaining technical details and budget considerations for PC components with enthusiasm.

White PC Case.description: A large, white, transparent-sided case wrapped in plastic, with visible internal fans and structure.

Corsair H100i RGB Platinum Box.description: Black box with a white and blue cooler image and 'H100i' branding.

Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box.description: White box with a futuristic design and 'B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE' printed on it, now visible as the motherboard itself with installed components.

First-Person Shooter Game Footage.description: In-game footage from a desert-themed map, showing a player holding a weapon and looking through an archway, with a minimap and score overlay.

Assembled White PC.description: A fully built white PC with RGB lighting, featuring a liquid cooler, multiple fans, and clean cable management, shown in a close-up shot.

Enermax Aquafusion ADX Box.description: Black box with 'AQUAFUSION ADX' branding and images of RGB fans and a liquid cooler, now represented by the user manual being referenced during installation.

CPU Cooler.description: A silver aluminum heatsink with a black retention mechanism, referred to by the speaker as a 'water cooler,' being installed onto the Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard with detailed verbal instructions on lifting latches, bringing up brackets, and securing the CPU.

User Manual.description: White paper booklet with 'ENERMAX AQUAFUSION ADX' branding and diagrams, being held and flipped through by the person to guide the CPU cooler installation.

G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM.description: White RAM sticks with black accents and 'G.SKILL' branding, being installed into the motherboard's RAM slots, identified by the man as having a speed of 6000 MHz with a CAS latency of 30, costing around 100 bucks for the kit, and chosen for its optimal performance-to-price ratio for a Ryzen system.

Small Component in Clear Plastic Bag.description: A small, unidentifiable component (possibly a heatsink or bracket) held in a transparent plastic bag by the man as he continues his explanation.

M.2 SSD: T-Force Cardea A440 Pro.description: A black and red 2TB M.2 NVMe SSD with 'T-FORCE' and 'CARDEA A440 PRO' branding, shown both in hand and installed on the motherboard, explicitly named by the man as a 'PCI Gen 4.0 SSD, 2 terabytes,' and described as a 'high quality, premium M.2 SSD' that offers good value without overspending, with its 'read and write speed' being a key feature.

Transparent Plastic Case Component.description: A clear, rigid plastic piece, likely a cover or part of the PC case, held and examined by the man during the segment.

raw_segments: The video opens with a black screen before an upbeat electronic music track begins, accompanying the transition to a man standing behind a table filled with computer hardware boxes against a vibrant blue background. Wearing a dark t-shirt, he speaks directly to the camera, introducing himself and the 'Skydive channel,' initially stating they will build the 'best $3,000 gaming PC in 2023.' An off-screen voice then interjects, questioning, 'Wait, I thought you said 2000,' followed by a cartoonish 'boing' sound and a 'YAY!' sound effect. The man corrects himself, confirming they are building the 'best $2,000 gaming PC in 2023.' He gestures toward the components, which include a Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, an AMD Ryzen 7 CPU, an ASUS ROG Strix GeForce RTX 4090, a Corsair H100i RGB Platinum liquid cooler, a Seasonic PRIME 850W power supply, and a white PC case wrapped in plastic. He picks up the RTX 4090 box, then an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX box, placing them back down as he explains that this build offers 'the best of the best' without excessive spending. He elaborates that the CPU and GPU in this $2,000 gaming PC will 'crush it' for 4K gaming, even with ray tracing, and perform excellently for content creation and game streaming. The scene briefly cuts to a fully assembled white PC with RGB lighting, showcasing a clean, modern build with a liquid cooler and multiple fans, before returning to the man. Brief gameplay footage is then shown: first, a racing game with a white car on a rainy track, followed by a first-person shooter in a desert environment. The video concludes with the man back at the table, smiling and gesturing toward the components, as on-screen text briefly displays 'be building' and '2000', reinforcing the PC's versatility.

The video opens with a close-up of a hand using a yellow screwdriver to secure components inside a white PC case, accompanied by the text 'Build guide'. The scene transitions to a computer screen displaying the installation of AMD Software Adrenalin Edition, with 'Windows & Drivers installation' overlaid. This is followed by dynamic in-game footage of a racing game and a first-person shooter, labeled 'Gaming Benchmarks'. Throughout these initial visuals, a man with a clear, enthusiastic male voice explains that he will "show you guys how to put together this PC step-by-step, the parts going inside of it, how to install Windows, drivers, and then the 4K gaming benchmarks," concluding with his "final thoughts on if you should build this $2000 computer." The scene then shifts to the Man, a young man with dark hair wearing a dark t-shirt, standing behind a table laden with various computer components, including the Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box, AMD Ryzen 7 CPU Box, ASUS ROG Strix GeForce RTX 4090 Box, Corsair H100i RGB Platinum Box, Seasonic PRIME 850W Power Supply Box, and a White PC Case wrapped in plastic. He gestures toward the components while speaking, and the text 'Links in the description' appears. He continues, stating that "links to all of the parts I use in this build guide can be found in the description below," encouraging viewers to "price check anything" or "check out the parts yourself." He then humorously adds, "to build this $2000 computer, all you need is a banana, scissors, and an 8 gigabyte or bigger USB flash drive." The Man picks up the AMD Ryzen 7 CPU Box and uses scissors to open it, with a distinct cutting sound, before holding up the CPU in its protective packaging. The text 'CPU: Ryzen 7 7800X3D link in description' is displayed. He elaborates on the CPU, identifying it as the "AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D," and confidently declares it "the best gaming CPU you can build, or you can get in 2023," emphasizing that "you don't need more than 8 cores for even a high-end gaming PC." The background features a solid blue with vertical white light tubes, creating a clean, tech-focused atmosphere, and an Assembled White PC is visible in the background during the CPU showcase.

The video opens with a young man, the speaker, with dark hair and a dark t-shirt, standing behind a table against a vibrant blue backdrop illuminated by vertical white light tubes. He begins by holding up a small, transparent package, likely a RAM stick, while speaking about the 7800X3D CPU, highlighting its optimal performance for a gaming PC due to its eight cores, 3D V-Cache, and ability to achieve high frame rates. He then places it down and picks up a black box labeled 'AQUAFUSION ADX' by Enermax, showcasing it to the camera as he explains that the 7800X3D doesn't come with a stock CPU cooler because it's a high-end CPU. The scene cuts to a close-up of a white PC case with internal RGB lighting, where a hand is seen installing the Enermax 240mm AIO liquid cooler onto a motherboard, with the 'ENERMAX' logo glowing. The video returns to the man, who now holds the Enermax box again, explaining that this specific white cooler fits the build's theme, while mentioning other alternatives are linked in the description. He then picks up the white 'Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box' and displays it, explicitly naming it the 'Gigabyte AORUS B650 Elite AX Ice' and confirming it has Wi-Fi, with on-screen text also reading 'MOBO: B650 Aorus Elite AX ICE link in description'. The camera then shows a close-up of the motherboard being installed into the white PC case, with the rear I/O panel visible. The man is then seen holding the motherboard, having removed it from its packaging, as he prepares to 'unsheathe' it, accompanied by the distinct sound of crinkling plastic.

The video opens with an upbeat electronic music track as a close-up reveals a Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard inside a white PC case, illuminated by vibrant blue and purple RGB lighting, highlighting its 'B650 AORUS' branding and 'Socket AM5' text. The camera pans across the motherboard, showcasing its sleek white and silver design, including the 'TEAM UP. FIGHT ON.' slogan. A young man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, then appears behind a table against a blue-lit backdrop, stating, "That's pretty cool. This is an all-white B650 motherboard. It's going to be more than enough for our 7800X3D and wow, this is a beauty." He unboxes the Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard from its white box, revealing accessories like a Wi-Fi antenna, a "handy little front IO adapter," and a QR code sheet for the manual, which he describes. He then holds up a small black component, the 7800X3D CPU, and displays it with the motherboard, announcing, "Now let's go ahead and install the 7800X3D onto our motherboard." With meticulous precision, his hands are shown carefully installing the CPU into the motherboard’s socket, securing it with a retention mechanism. He explains the need to "unscrew these brackets" and notes that "since this is a 7800X3D... it doesn't come with a stock cooler." He proceeds to fasten the mounting screws for a water cooler, specifically mentioning an "Enermax AIO," using a screwdriver around the heatsink, maintaining focus on the clean, white aesthetic of the components and the careful handling of each part.

The segment opens with a close-up of hands installing a silver aluminum heatsink CPU cooler, which the clear, instructional male voice of the speaker refers to as a "water cooler," onto a white and silver Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, visibly branded 'AORUS ELITE AX ICE'. The person carefully places the cooler onto the CPU socket and secures it with screws, following the speaker's verbal instructions to "lift up this latch," "bring up this bracket," and "unsheath your CPU," ensuring alignment of a triangle on the CPU with one on the socket. The speaker advises referring to "the manual" for detailed installation steps, which is visually confirmed as the person picks up and flips through the Enermax Aquafusion ADX user manual. After consulting the manual, the cooler is properly aligned and fastened, with the speaker guiding to "bring back down this bracket" and "pull down this lever" before a final "bam." The scene transitions to the person holding the motherboard upright, showcasing the newly installed CPU cooler. The video then cuts to a close-up of white G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM sticks being installed into the motherboard’s RAM slots. The speaker identifies these as "a 32-gigabyte kit of RAM from G.Skill," specifically their "S5 series," echoing the on-screen text 'G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM 32GB'. The final frames show the RAM installed alongside the CPU cooler on the motherboard, all set against a blue-lit, tech-focused background.

The video opens with a close-up of a white G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM stick being installed into a motherboard, set against a sleek blue-lit background. A young man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, then appears behind a component-laden table, speaking directly to the camera with clear, professional speech. He explains his choice of RAM, noting that "pretty much anything is going to be universal with this Ryzen system," but specifically chose this RAM because "it's white" and boasts a "speed of 6000 MHz with a CAS latency of 30," which is "really nice." He reveals he acquired the RAM kit for "about 100 bucks," considering it a "pretty nice deal," and advises against spending more for "minimal FPS gains," concluding they are "making the most of it with our budget with these two sticks of RAM." He then transitions to discussing SSDs, holding up a silver M.2 SSD, identified on-screen as the 'T-Force Cardea A440 Pro 2TB'. He places it on the table, picks up and examines a transparent plastic case component, and then displays multiple M.2 SSDs side by side. The man introduces the "Team Group T-Force Cardera A440 Pro" as a "PCI Gen 4.0 SSD, 2 terabytes," comparing its value to the RAM by stating it's "as expensive as you'd want to go for an M.2 SSD without really overspending," while still being a "high quality, premium M.2 SSD." A close-up shot shows the T-Force SSD being installed into the Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, with on-screen text confirming 'M.2 SSD: T-Force Cardea A440 Pro'. The man returns to the table, holding a small component in a clear plastic bag, continuing his explanation about "read and write speed." The scene maintains a professional, tech-focused atmosphere with bright vertical LED strips casting a cool blue glow, and the table remains cluttered with various parts and tools.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a dark t-shirt, illuminated by vertical white light tubes against a blue backdrop, begins by holding up a component and explaining that M.2 SSDs, while fast, are an excellent value around $100. The Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard box, an AMD Ryzen 7 CPU box, and a silver water bottle are on the table. He then demonstrates installing a white G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM stick into the motherboard, instructing viewers to use RAM slots A2 and B2, the furthest from the CPU, and to press firmly until a click is heard, which is confirmed by a distinct sound. He installs a second RAM stick similarly. Using a small screwdriver, he tightens screws on the silver aluminum CPU cooler, securing it. On-screen text identifies the RAM. Preparing for M.2 SSD installation, he switches to a smaller Phillips head screwdriver bit for the smaller screws. His hands then meticulously install a black and red T-Force Cardea A440 Pro M.2 SSD onto the Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, already inside a white PC case, explaining its single orientation due to a notch and confirming its seating with a click. He attaches a silver aluminum heatsink with a black retention bracket over the SSD, securing it with screws, accompanied by screwdriver sounds. After a final screw turn and a satisfied 'Sweet,' the scene widens to show the man gesturing to the open white PC case, identifying it as the 'Montech King 95 Pro' for a '$2000 gaming PC build.' The video concludes with a cinematic view of the fully assembled white PC, featuring blue RGB lighting, clean cable management, and a liquid cooler, with on-screen text confirming the case model and a link in the description.

event_blocks.summary: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, begins by unboxing the Montech King 95 PC case accessory box, revealing a container of screws, a case manual, a white plastic drive cage, and a transparent plastic sheet for additional SSDs. He notes the case comes with six pre-installed RGB fans and mentions an alternative mesh front panel for better airflow. The Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard box is visible on the table. The man then opens the white PC case, the Montech King 95 Pro, to reveal its internal structure and installed white fans. He explains how to gain access to the interior by unscrewing parts and notes the pre-installed standoffs on the motherboard tray. He aligns the Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard with the rear IO opening and secures it with screws from the accessory box. The process continues with the installation of the CPU Cooler, G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM into the motherboard slots, and the M.2 SSD: T-Force Cardea A440 Pro into its M.2 slot. He then demonstrates a criss-cross tightening method for the motherboard screws to ensure even pressure. The man introduces the Montech Century G5 850W 80 PLUS Gold power supply, highlighting its modularity and suitability for high-end components. He unboxes the PSU, placing it into the PC case and managing its cables, including the CPU power cable and a chunky motherboard power connector. He also connects a SATA power cable for the case's RGB and fan controller and a PCIe power cable for the graphics card. The man details the front IO cables, including the power button, reset button, HD audio, USB Type-C, and USB 3.0 connectors, praising Montech's single-connector design for the front panel header. He connects the HD audio connector to the motherboard, emphasizing its one-way insertion and advising to consult the manual. The T-Force Cardea A440 Pro M.2 SSD is installed, followed by the connection of the 5-volt ARGB header for the fans and the single system fan header. The front panel header is connected to the 'F_PANEL' header on the motherboard, with the speaker noting its convenient single-connector design. The motherboard features an AORUS SERIES heatsink and a CMOS battery, with a white Montech fan visible.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of the fully assembled Montech King 95 Pro PC Case, glowing with blue RGB lighting and showcasing its clean internal layout with white fans and organized cabling. On-screen text identifies the case. A young man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, then appears seated behind a table against a vibrant blue backdrop. He immediately begins explaining that this is the "Pro version" of the case, highlighting its "six fans pre-included" which are "all RGB" and "pre-hooked up to the back of the case," saving a lot of work. He also notes the base model is "like 120" and a "pretty nice value." He then starts unboxing a brown cardboard box labeled 'MONTECH KING 95', referring to its contents as the "accessory box." From it, he removes a small plastic container with screws and small parts, explaining it contains "all the screws we'd ever need." Next, he pulls out a white paper manual, which he calls the "Case manual," advising "first-time PC builder" to "please follow this." He then extracts a white plastic drive cage and a large, transparent plastic sheet, which he holds up and examines, describing it as "additional storage accessory stuff for putting in additional SSDs." The white PC case, now without its side panel, sits to his right, revealing its internal structure and two white fans. He continues to unpack, mentioning a "special type of front panel that has open mesh" for "more airflow" as an alternative to the "glass version" of the case, though for this build, they'll keep it in its "stock form." The Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box is visible on the left side of the table, alongside other tools and components, indicating a larger PC build in progress.

The video begins with the Man, a young man with dark hair wearing a dark t-shirt, seated behind a table against a vibrant blue backdrop, introducing the PC build process in a clear, instructional tone. He gestures toward the open White PC Case, identified as the Montech King 95 Pro, which is positioned prominently, revealing its internal structure with two white fans already installed. He explains that he has "unscrewed" parts and can "press this out, take it out" to gain "access to the inside of the PC case." The Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box is visible to his left. The Man then opens the case further, revealing the motherboard installed inside, with the CPU socket exposed and the AORUS branding clearly visible. He notes that "all the standoffs [are] pre-installed for our motherboard" and, referring to the large size of the "King 95" case, states, "you can literally just drop it in." He describes his method of aligning the motherboard by lining up "the rear IO here with the opening on the back of the King 95," which allows him to "screw in our motherboard screws." event_blocks.summary: A man in a dark t-shirt explains the fan RGB connections on a white Montech King 95 Pro PC case, detailing ARGB header daisy-chaining. He then introduces the ASRock TAICHI WHITE GRAPHICS CARD, specifically the Radeon RX 7900 XTX with 24GB VRAM, acquired for under $1000, highlighting its value compared to the RTX 4090 and the rarity of white editions. He removes a drive bay cage using a yellow-handled screwdriver, secures the GPU with screws, and audibly confirms its firm installation with a click. The PC boots to the AMI BIOS screen, where he instructs to reset the FTPM by pressing 'Y', leading into Windows Setup. The process continues with signing into a Microsoft account, potentially restoring data from 'TheGigaPC', and setting up as a new PC, culminating in the creation of a login PIN. The man speaks directly to the camera, detailing the graphics card's high-performance capabilities, stating it "can honestly do it all" for "4K gaming, ray tracing, high frame rate 1440p, high frame rate, high graphics, 4K," and for "streaming and other content creation ideas." He emphasizes its value, noting that for "under $1,000," it offers "24 gigabytes of VRAM," matching the more expensive RTX 4090. As he speaks, the scene briefly transitions to cyberpunk-themed gameplay footage, showing a character in a neon-lit, rain-slicked alleyway with an on-screen announcer and subtitles, before cutting back to the man. He then proceeds to unbox the ASRock TAICHI WHITE Radeon RX 7900 XTX GPU, a sleek white card with three fans and RGB lighting, with audible rustling sounds of packaging. He carefully removes the GPU from its anti-static bag, showcasing its design, and deliberately installs it into the clean White PC Case, which already features white cables and a liquid cooler. The man continues to explain the importance of hardware, stating, "you can never truly upgrade the hardware" as "what comes with this graphics card will forever be soldered onto the PCP." Close-up shots of the installed GPU feature on-screen text: 'GPU: Radeon RX 7900 XTX link in description', all under consistent cool blue lighting that highlights the white components and RGB effects.

The Man, dressed in a dark t-shirt, begins by explaining the permanence of VRAM in graphics cards, specifically the RX 7000 series like the 7900 XTX, stating that the hardware is "really good for the money." He holds up the white ASRock TAICHI WHITE GRAPHICS CARD box, prominently featuring 'AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX' and a red '24GB' label, before unboxing the white ASRock TAICHI Radeon RX 7900 XTX GPU, showcasing its triple-fan design and 'TAICHI' branding to the camera against a vibrant blue backdrop. With the White PC Case: Montech King 95 Pro, already partially assembled with internal blue RGB lighting and white Montech fans, sitting to his right, he instructs viewers on how to install the "three-slot GPU." He then proceeds to remove a drive bay cage from the case using a yellow-handled screwdriver, advising to "save these screws for later," accompanied by the distinct sounds of unscrewing and metal clinking. Afterward, he carefully aligns and inserts the ASRock TAICHI WHITE Radeon RX 7900 XTX GPU into the topmost PCIe slot on the Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard, which is already installed inside the case, saying, "put it in here, line it up." He secures the GPU with screws, ensuring it is firmly in place. Throughout the process, the case’s clean cable management and white internal components are visible, with the GPU’s white backplate and sleek design contrasting against the blue lighting. On-screen text appears, reading 'GPU: Radeon RX 7900 XTX link in description', providing product information. He gives the GPU a "firm press" and a distinct click is heard, signifying its secure installation, before gesturing toward the completed step with satisfaction.

The segment opens with a close-up of the Man, wearing a dark t-shirt, meticulously installing the ASRock TAICHI WHITE Radeon RX 7900 XTX GPU into the White PC Case: Montech King 95 Pro. His steady hands align the GPU with the motherboard and secure it, as he audibly instructs, "screw this back into the case so it doesn't sag." He then connects the necessary power cables, explaining, "we're going to go ahead and plug in each of these PCIE power connectors into the graphics card one by one," emphasizing to "make sure each of these firmly go in." The interior of the case glows with blue RGB lighting, highlighting the white Montech Fans and liquid cooling system, with "GPU: Radeon RX 7900 XTX link in description" text overlayed. After successfully connecting the cables, the Man exclaims, "There we go. That is in," and with a satisfied "Woo! Alright, we did it," he proudly announces, "You successfully built a $2000 gaming PC." The scene transitions to the Man, now in a white t-shirt, seated at a desk with the fully assembled White PC to his right. He holds a small black USB device, which he identifies as a "Windows bootable media device" or "8 gigabyte or bigger flash drive," as he explains the next step: "now let's install Windows on it." He guides viewers through a "pre-boot checklist," instructing them to have the USB device "on hand, ready to go," and mentions a video tutorial for creating one. A close-up then shows a hand inserting a USB device into the back of a black PC case, illustrating the Man's instruction to "plug into the computer for when we need to install Windows." The lighting shifts from the vibrant blue of the build to a more subdued, professional studio setup, marking the transition from assembly to the software installation phase.

The video transitions to the final PC setup, where the Man, now in a white t-shirt, is seated at a black desk with a colorful mousepad, black keyboard, and monitor. He begins by instructing viewers to ensure their "keyboard and your mouse are plugged into your computer," and that a "display cable" runs "from the monitor to where your graphics card is in your PC," emphasizing not to plug it into the motherboard. He then advises to "supply power to your monitor, so it actually turns on." Visually, he connects a black power cable to the rear of the Assembled White PC, a Montech King 95 Pro with internal RGB lighting and a transparent side panel, explaining to plug it into "the back of the computer where the power supply is," specifically the 'TOUGHPower GF3 750W' PSU. He instructs to "flip the switch from the zero to the one" on the PC, then takes a small black USB device, identifying it as a "Windows bootable media device," and plugs it into the front USB port, noting he's putting it "in the front because that's what I feel like doing." After pressing the power button, the monitor displays the AMI BIOS screen, prompting the Man to exclaim "Ooh!" and guide the viewer, saying "If you see something like this, just go ahead and proceed." He explains they will "reset the FTPM" by pressing 'Y' on the keyboard. As the PC boots into Windows Setup, showing 'Setup is starting' and prompting for Windows activation, the Man points at the screen, indicating the next steps, stating, "There's a little circle here, and now let's go ahead and install Windows," concluding with "All right, let's go ahead and install Windows. So hit install now." The well-lit, modern environment maintains focus on the Man's instructional interaction with the PC setup.

The segment opens with a male voice guiding the Windows 11 installation on a large monitor, instructing the viewer to either enter a product key or skip it, which he does. He then directs the selection of the Windows 11 Home 64-bit version and proceeds through installation type, confirming the drive. The instructional tone continues as he prompts the user to choose their country (United States is visible), keyboard layout (US), and language. A hand gestures towards the screen, emphasizing these selections. The narrator then explains connecting to the internet, stating "I'm going to go ahead and connect to my internet here," before hitting "next." He advises on naming the device but opts to skip it, then moves to signing into a Microsoft account, explaining how to use a previous account from "Xbox Live" or a current computer, or to create a new one. The screen visually progresses through naming the device, adding a Microsoft account, and showing an option to restore data from 'TheGigaPC'. The instructor then guides to "click more options" and "set up as a new PC" if presented, concluding with the instruction to "create the login we're going to use." The monitor, in a well-lit room, clearly displays the Microsoft logo, Wi-Fi symbol, and various app icons like Edge, Xbox, and OneDrive throughout the instructional setup process, including a progress indicator for PIN setup, though the PIN selection itself isn't explicitly narrated in this minute.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a male narrator guiding the user through Windows 11 setup, advising to disable unnecessary features and decline extra offers, transitioning to the Windows 11 desktop on a black monitor where a hand gestures. The narrator announces they are on the home screen and will now install graphics and chipset drivers. A web browser opens, and the narrator instructs to search for 'Radeon drivers' and navigate to the 'AMD Drivers and Support Page.' Identifying the graphics card as the '7900 XTX,' the hand selects the AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX, and the narrator directs the user to find the 'Windows 11 version,' suggesting either 'auto-detect and install' or the 'Adrenalin Edition' for download. Concurrently, the narrator instructs to search for the motherboard, the 'Aorus B650 Elite AX Ice,' leading to the Gigabyte Support Page, identified as the 'motherboard product page.' The focus shifts to the Gigabyte support page for the B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE motherboard, where the narrator instructs to click on 'support' and then 'utility.' A hand navigates to the driver download section, and the narrator highlights the 'Gigabyte Control Center full installation package' as a beneficial alternative to individual driver downloads. The hand selects the download, and a permission pop-up appears, which the narrator instructs to 'Click yes.' After the download begins, the user opens the file, and the narrator guides the installation of the 'graphics card driver installation software,' referring to the AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition installer, noting its red and black design and showing progress. The narrator states, 'There you go, there's our graphics card install, let this run and let it do its thing.' While the graphics driver installs, the narrator instructs to 'open up the Gigabyte Control Center file' and 'extract all of this,' with the hand interacting with the screen. After extraction, the narrator says 'let's run' and 'hit yes.' The Gigabyte Control Center installation window appears, showing a license agreement against the Windows 11 default blue abstract wallpaper, with the taskbar visible. The narrator observes that the 'graphics card driver is finished installing, but we're not going to restart just yet,' instructing to 'hit finish' as the Gigabyte Control Center installation is 'finishing up.' A black cookie consent banner remains visible at the bottom of the browser window throughout the process.

Monitor.description: A black monitor displaying the Gigabyte support page for the B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE motherboard, with a browser window open to the driver download section, showing various software packages and their details, all while a male voice provides step-by-step instructions for navigation and installation.

Web Browser.description: A browser window displaying multiple tabs, including the Gigabyte support page, with visible search bars, navigation menus, and download buttons for drivers, showing the 'B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE' product page with a driver download section, which the male speaker explicitly guides the viewer to navigate.

Gigabyte Support Page.description: A webpage with a clean white layout, displaying the 'B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE' product page, including a driver download section with version, size, date, and download links for various components, such as the GIGABYTE Control Center Full Installation Package, which the male speaker specifically names and instructs the user to download.

AMD Drivers & Support Page.description: A webpage with a black header, displaying options for Windows 10/11 and Linux drivers, a product selection dropdown, and a download button for the AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX drivers. The narrator specifically mentions selecting the '7900 XTX' and finding the 'Windows 11 version' for 'auto-detect and install' or 'Adrenalin Edition.'

Hand.description: A human hand, appearing from the left side of the frame, gesturing toward the monitor to indicate interaction with the setup screens, pointing at options like driver selection and download buttons, guiding the viewer through the process in direct response to the male speaker's verbal instructions.

Cookie Consent Banner.description: A black banner at the bottom of the browser window, stating 'This website uses cookies to provide you a more personalized and responsive service. By using this website you agree to our use of cookies. If you require more information or prefer not to accept cookies please visit our Privacy Policy.' with a checkmark icon.

GIGABYTE CONTROL CENTER.description: An installation window with a dark blue background, displaying the Gigabyte Control Center license agreement, with 'Accept' and 'Cancel' buttons, and a blue abstract wallpaper in the background, which the male speaker frequently names, describes its purpose, and guides the user through its installation process.

AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition Installe.description: A pop-up window with a red and black design, displaying the AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition installer, showing progress and a welcome screen, with options to 'Install' or 'Cancel', which the male speaker refers to as the "graphics card driver installation software" and guides the user to install.

raw_segments: The video opens with a male voice narrating the Windows 11 setup process, advising to 'tick them to off' for unnecessary features and to 'skip or decline' extra offers for PC games. The visual transitions from the setup to the Windows 11 desktop environment, where a hand gestures on the black monitor. The narrator announces, 'All right, we are on the Windows home screen,' and states the next step is to 'install graphics card drivers and our chipset drivers.' The hand then opens a web browser, and the narrator instructs to 'type in Radeon drivers' and 'click on AMD Drivers and Support.' The browser navigates to the AMD Drivers & Support Page, where the narrator explains they already know the graphics card is the '7900 XTX.' The hand selects the AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX, and the narrator guides the viewer to find the 'Windows 11 version' and either 'auto-detect and install' or use the 'Adrenalin Edition,' initiating the download. The hand searches for 'gigabyte b650 elite ax ice drivers,' leading to the Gigabyte Support Page, which the narrator identifies as the 'motherboard product page for it,' ready for further driver downloads.

The video opens with a close-up of a black monitor displaying the Gigabyte support page for the B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE motherboard. A male voice instructs the viewer to "Click on support," then "click on utility," as a human hand, appearing from the left, gestures and navigates the web browser to the driver download section. The speaker highlights the "Gigabyte Control Center full installation package," explaining its benefit over individually downloading each driver. The hand points to and selects the download, prompting a pop-up window for permission, which the speaker instructs to "Click yes." The speaker then guides the installation of the "graphics card driver installation software," referring to the AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition installer that appears with its red and black design, showing progress and a welcome screen. The speaker says, "There you go, there's our graphics card install, let this run and let it do its thing." While that runs, the speaker instructs to "open up the Gigabyte Control Center file" and "extract all of this," as the hand interacts with the screen. After extracting, the speaker says "let's run" and "hit yes." The installation window for the Gigabyte Control Center then appears, displaying a license agreement against the Windows 11 default blue abstract wallpaper, with the taskbar visible. The speaker notes that "our graphics card driver is finished installing, but we're not going to restart just yet," instructing to "hit finish" as the Gigabyte Control Center installation is "finishing up." event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a Man's instructional voice guiding viewers through the Gigabyte Control Center software on a Monitor, checking available updates for drivers like Realtek audio and AMD Chipset. A Hand gestures, selecting necessary drivers before initiating the installation process. Following a system restart, the Man directs the viewer to the BIOS Interface, specifically the AORUS Easy Mode, to enable the XMP profile for RAM, concluding the setup. The system then boots into a vibrant racing game, showcasing a white Bentley race car on a rain-slicked Hockenheim track. The Man, visible in a small inset, explains he's running the game maxed out at 4K, detailing graphics settings like 'Ultra' and enabling 'AMD FSR 2.0' for increased FPS. He comments on the car's performance, noting good brakes and his connection with them, while the HUD displays fluctuating FPS between 89-125 and GPU temperatures around 65-67°C. The gameplay features dynamic weather effects, engine sounds, tire squeals, and rain, with the Man occasionally gesturing and reacting to the race, finding the AI too easy. Notifications for 'Overtake' and 'Level Up' appear as the Bentley navigates the track, with the Man observing the car's 'squirming' movement and the game progressing through laps.

Racing Game Footage.description: In-game footage of a white Bentley race car with 'Mobil 1 SERVICE CENTRE' branding on a rainy, foggy racetrack, showing real-time performance metrics (FPS 92–113, GPU 65–67°C), lap timer (00:00.00–00:57.00), and notifications for overtakes and level-up rewards, with rain effects and reflections on the wet road. The footage is accompanied by realistic racing game sounds, including engine revving, tire squeals, and the distinct patter of rain, creating an immersive auditory experience.

Monitor.description: A black monitor displaying the racing game's settings menu and gameplay, transitioning from the 'DISPLAY' tab with resolution and FSR 2.0 settings to the in-game third-person racing view with HUD elements and performance overlays.

Man.description: A young man with dark hair, wearing a white t-shirt and earphones, seated at a desk, observing the gameplay in a small circular inset in the top right corner, occasionally gesturing with his right hand (pointing, thumbs-up) to react to the race. He speaks in a clear voice, narrating the process of adjusting game settings, explaining his reasoning for changes (e.g., enabling FSR for more FPS), commenting on the game's performance, reacting to the gameplay, and critiquing the AI's difficulty.

BIOS Interface.description: The AORUS Easy Mode BIOS screen displaying system information such as CPU frequency (4204.55 MHz), memory frequency (4890.36 MT/s), CPU temperature (28.0°C), and RAM status, with options to enable XMP/EXPO and save settings, where the Man's voice guides the user to enable the XMP profile for RAM and then save and exit.

Desk Setup.description: A black desk with a colorful mousepad, a black keyboard, a mouse, and a monitor, forming the workspace for the Man during the explanation and setup phase, with the Assembled White PC placed to the right.

Hand.description: A human hand entering from the left side of the frame, gesturing toward the monitor to indicate interaction with the Gigabyte Control Center, pointing at specific updates, and clicking the 'Install' button.

GIGABYTE CONTROL CENTER.description: An application window showing the 'Available Updates' page with a list of drivers and software, including Norton Internet Security, Realtek drivers, AMD Chipset Driver, and AMD Ryzen Master, with 'Install' and 'Exit' buttons at the bottom, whose contents are verbally analyzed by the Man, who specifies which updates are necessary.

Assembled White PC.description: A fully built white PC with RGB lighting, featuring a transparent side panel, internal blue and multicolor lighting, and a 'TOUGHPower GF3 750W' PSU, shown during the power connection and boot process, now operational and displaying the game.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of the Monitor displaying the Gigabyte Control Center software loading, as a Man's clear, instructional voice explains they're checking "what software is currently available." A Hand enters, gesturing as the software transitions to the 'Available Updates' page, listing drivers like Norton Internet Security, Realtek audio, AMD Chipset Driver, and AMD Ryzen Master. The Man's voice guides the viewer, stating they "don't need Norton Internet Security" or "AMD Ryzen Master," but do need "AMD Chipset drivers," "Realtek audio drivers," and "the WiFi driver for our WiFi stick and Bluetooth." The Hand points to specific updates, indicating selections, before clicking 'Install' as the Man instructs to "let this go ahead and run." The screen then shows a 'Restarting' message, and the Man announces they'll "restart the computer" to do "one last thing in the BIOS." After the restart, the BIOS Interface appears, showing the AORUS Easy Mode with system information. The Man's voice instructs to "press the delete key on your keyboard when the logo of the motherboard comes up" to enter the BIOS, and then to "go ahead and enable your XMP profile for your RAM," before clicking save and exit, declaring "you are done." The system boots into Windows, transitioning to vibrant Racing Game Footage of a white race car on a rainy track with performance metrics. A small circular inset appears in the top right, showing the Man, now in a white t-shirt, holding a small black USB device and observing the gameplay, as his voice excitedly asks, "Alright, what about Forza Motorsport?" The Desk Setup and Assembled White PC remain consistent, now fully operational and displaying the game.

The video opens with a third-person rear-view perspective of a white Bentley race car, branded 'Mobil 1 SERVICE CENTRE', navigating a wet, rain-slicked Hockenheim racetrack in a racing game, a track the player notes "just got released." Raindrops streak across the screen, reducing visibility and creating reflective surfaces, as the player comments, "the game was like, 'yo, let's put you on rainy graphics,' 'cause why not?" The car is positioned behind a red Corvette and a black race car, with several other colorful vehicles ahead. The environment is foggy and overcast, with trackside barriers, grandstands, and distant trees barely visible. In the top right, a young man with dark hair, wearing a white t-shirt and earphones, is seated at a desk, observing the gameplay with focused attention. He speaks throughout, describing how they are "running this game completely maxed out at 4K FPS," noting the FPS display is "so small" his "editor's gonna have to enlarge that." He identifies his car as the "Boatley" (Bentley), praising its "great brakes" and his "good connection with the brakes." On-screen HUD elements display FPS fluctuating between 92 and 113, GPU temperature around 65–67°C, and the current lap timer progressing from 00:00.00 to 00:57.00, showing 'LAP 1/2'. As the player accelerates through straightaways and navigates turns, guided by blue directional arrows, he expresses frustration, "Why am I in first gear? No, no, on a wet circuit. Nope," and acknowledges, "cars kind of all over the place." He also notes difficulty hearing the car's need to "upshift." Notifications for '+10 EXP Overtake' and '+200 CP Level Up Reward' appear as he successfully overtakes a red car and a black Red Bull car. The track features overhead banners like 'SPLI SECOND' and 'REVENOL', and the car passes under a bridge structure. The gameplay is smooth, with consistent frame rates and dynamic weather effects, accompanied by the sounds of racing engines, tire squeals, and rain. The man in the inset remains engaged, occasionally gesturing with his right hand (pointing, thumbs-up), reacting subtly to the race’s progression, and even laughing at one point while anticipating a corner. The in-game car maintains a steady position in the pack, approaching the end of the first lap, as the man's commentary cuts off.

The segment opens with a young man, wearing a white t-shirt and earphones, observing gameplay in a top-right circular inset, subtly gesturing. He then transitions to the in-game settings menu of a racing game, explaining in a clear voice, "we're going to show you guys the graphics settings real quick. Just show I show you guys how mental this computer is." He navigates the 'DISPLAY' tab, confirming 'Dynamic render quality' is 'Ultra' and 'Resolution scale' is '100%', stating, "Right, we got ultra settings. 100% render resolution. Everything's on auto 'cause it's on ultra." Noticing 'AMD FSR 2.0' is off, he decides to enable it for "more FPS," setting the 'Resolution' to '3840x2160' and 'Quality' mode, while on-screen text shows VRAM usage. As the game resumes with a third-person view of a white Bentley race car branded 'Mobil 1 SERVICE CENTRE' on a foggy, rain-slicked track, the man comments on the performance, "now we are running at about a hundred... I got no complaints," as the HUD displays FPS fluctuating between 89–125 and GPU temperature at 65–67°C. The immersive sounds of engine revs, tire squeals, and rain accompany the Bentley as it maneuvers through turns, overtaking other vehicles amidst the mist and reflections on the wet asphalt. The man continues to observe, occasionally gesturing, narrating his driving ("Just gotta find traction coming out of these slow corners") and critiquing the AI, finding it "so slow" and "hilarious," even suggesting "the AI is too easy." Notifications for 'Overtake' and '+10 EXP' appear as the lap timer progresses to 00:57.00, with the man concluding his observations by noting the car's "squirming" movement.

event_blocks.summary: The minute opens with in-game footage of a white Bentley race car with 'Mobil 1 SERVICE CENTRE' branding navigating a rainy, foggy racetrack, displaying real-time performance metrics and a lap timer, while the Man, visible in a circular inset, enthusiastically narrates his gameplay, exclaiming about a "beautiful move" and going "off track" before deciding to "try another game." The scene briefly transitions to a first-person shooter game set in a futuristic, sterile environment, with the Man continuing to react visually. The gameplay then shifts to the settings menu of 'Starfield', where the Man, speaking with an enthusiastic and explanatory tone, highlights the game's "stunning" visuals at "4K" and "Ultra settings," despite its "questionable" performance, adjusting display options like 'Upscaling' (FSR2) and 'Render Resolution Scale' (set to 100%), emphasizing turning on "sharpening and film grain" and "Depth of field," demonstrating the Assembled White PC's capability to run the game very well, with FPS fluctuating between 165 and 154, all displayed on his black monitor as he gestures towards the screen.

The Man's voice narrates the action, celebrating a 'beautiful move' and acknowledging going 'off track' before deciding to switch games.

Monitor.description: A black monitor displaying the racing game's settings menu and gameplay, transitioning from the 'DISPLAY' tab with resolution and FSR 2.0 settings to the in-game third-person racing view with HUD elements and performance overlays, which the Man directly references in his commentary about game performance and settings.

The Man's commentary on 'Starfield's' high-quality performance ('can you run it very well on this computer') directly showcases the capabilities of this PC.

event_blocks.summary: The first-person perspective opens within a brightly lit, futuristic spaceship corridor, near a large circular airlock door marked 'BEWARE PRESSURIZED' and 'KEEP CLEAR'. The Man, observed in a circular inset, remarks on the game's performance, noting "80 frames per second indoors" and discussing V-sync. As the player approaches the airlock, it opens, revealing a dimly lit, red-lit cockpit area labeled 'RAZORLEAF' with 'BOARD' and 'COCKPIT' prompts. The Man expresses admiration for the "detail there is in every single thing in the ship," specifically praising the clarity of the on-screen text, especially on a 4K display, and reiterating, "I can see the text, all the tiny things in the ship very well," attributing this to the "absolute detail" a 4K gaming PC offers. The player navigates through the cockpit, passing a desk with a globe and tools, then approaches a 'DEIMOS' bridge station designated 'PILOT SEAT' with a 'SIT' prompt. Upon sitting, the viewpoint shifts to a first-person cockpit perspective, showcasing a star-filled expanse outside the viewport. The player then initiates an undocking sequence through an interface displaying 'DOCKED' and options to 'UNDOCK' or 'GET UP'. The scene transitions to an external view of the spacecraft, now adrift in space, with the player character visible in the cockpit, poised for undocking. The Man concludes his commentary on the setup's value, stating, "to get this sort of clarity at $2,000? Not that bad really," before his speech is cut off. On-screen performance metrics, including FPS and GPU temperature, are visible in the top-left corner.

Gameplay Footage.description: In-game footage from a cyberpunk-themed game, showing a character walking through a neon-lit, rain-slicked alleyway with glowing signs, NPCs, and a heads-up display, including subtitles and an announcer's voiceover.

Man.description: A young man with dark hair, wearing a white t-shirt and earphones, seated at a desk, observing the gameplay in a small circular inset. He speaks enthusiastically and conversationally, providing a running commentary on the game's performance (mentioning '80 frames per second' and V-sync), the impressive visual 'detail' of the ship, the clarity of 'text' in 4K, and the value of the gaming setup, occasionally expressing awe with exclamations like 'Oh, man. Oh,' and gesturing subtly.

On-Screen Text.description: White text overlays like 'RAZORLEAF', 'BOARD', 'COCKPIT', 'PILOT SEAT', 'SIT', 'DOCKED', 'UNDOCK', 'GET UP', and system status indicators, which are specifically highlighted by the Man for their exceptional clarity and readability, even for 'tiny things', especially when viewed in 4K.

First-Person Shooter Game Footage.description: In-game footage from a futuristic, sterile environment, showing a player character in a white and black armored suit walking through high-tech corridors and a cockpit, interacting with airlocks and pilot seats. event_blocks.summary: The video begins with intense first-person shooter gameplay on a sunlit, Middle Eastern-inspired map, featuring sandy architecture and narrow alleys, where the player utilizes an M4A1-S rifle. The in-game Heads-Up Display (HUD) prominently shows performance metrics like FPS ranging from 312 to 327 and a GPU temperature of 69°C, alongside a mini-map and location identifiers such as 'Mid Doors' and 'Bombsite B'. The gameplay is accompanied by distinct audio cues of gunshots and player movement. A small, circular inset in the top right corner features the Man, a young man in a white t-shirt and earphones, who reacts to the game with subtle smiles and nods. He then comments on achieving "300 FPS gameplay at 4K" and declares, "Let's wrap up the build video." The scene shifts to a clean, minimalist studio environment with a white backdrop and vertical light bars. The Man is now positioned at a black desk, with the Assembled White PC, housed in a Montech King 95 Pro case featuring a transparent side panel, internal blue RGB lighting, and white Montech fans, situated beside him. He speaks with animated gestures, asserting that "this PC makes 4K gaming worth it," contrasting this with previous experiences where comparable performance would have cost approximately $3,000. The studio lighting alternates between white and blue, accentuating the PC's RGB illumination. The Man highlights the "very affordable" CPU and GPU combination for 4K gaming. A close-up shot then focuses on the black and orange AMD Ryzen 7 CPU box, presented by a hand before the illuminated PC, as the Man identifies the "best gaming CPU on the market," the "Ryzen 7 7800X3D," as costing between "$350 to $400." Continuing with hand gestures, he transitions to a close-up of the white ASRock TAICHI WHITE Radeon RX 7900 XTX GPU box, noting its "24GB" capacity. He describes this GPU as "the best high-end graphics card that isn't going to be just throwing tons of money away for only minimal gains," reinforcing the PC's value proposition. The segment concludes with the Man smiling and gesturing towards the camera, emphasizing the PC's visual appeal and performance capabilities. The video further explores the Assembled White PC's interior through a dynamic close-up, highlighting the ASRock TAICHI WHITE Radeon RX 7900 XTX GPU with its white shroud, RGB lighting, and neatly managed white cables, all bathed in vibrant blue and multicolor internal lighting. The camera smoothly pans across the meticulously organized build, drawing attention to the white Montech fans and premium components such as the G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM. Subsequently, the scene transitions to a medium shot of the Man, seated at his black desk within the minimalist studio featuring a white background and vertical light bars. Wearing a white t-shirt and a small microphone attached to his collar, he speaks with evident enthusiasm, explaining that the 7900 XTX represents a top-tier GPU option without excessive expenditure, stating it "still holds up even for just under $1000" due to its "so much raw performance and excellent built-in hardware," justifying its "$950 price tag for 4K gaming." While gesturing expressively and occasionally directing attention to the illuminated Assembled White PC to his left, he draws a critical comparison with the more expensive RTX 4080 and 4090, noting the 4080's retail price exceeding $1200 and the 4090's cost around $2400. He concludes that the 7900 XTX can rival or surpass the 4090 in raw performance at nearly half the price, his engaging presentation and consistent eye contact underscoring a professional and passionate review of the PC's aesthetic and technical merits.
